text: The spotlight metaphor of attention originates with Michael Posner's landmark 1980 paper on spatial cueing. Posner showed that directing covert attention — without moving the eyes — to a location in space speeds the detection of stimuli appearing there, exactly as a spotlight illuminates a patch of stage. What the metaphor also implies, and what subsequent research confirmed, is that the spotlight has an edge: stimuli just outside the attended region are actually suppressed below baseline, creating an "inhibitory surround." Attention is not simply a flashlight added to a dark room; it simultaneously brightens the center and dims the periphery.
question: What does the passage say happens to stimuli just outside the attended region in Posner's spotlight model?

options.text: They are processed at baseline speed, neither faster nor slower.
They receive extra processing to orient the spotlight toward them.
They are suppressed below baseline, forming an inhibitory surround.
They are redirected to the right hemisphere for parallel processing.
